event: David slays the lion to protect his flock
symbols: lamb cradled in arm — the helpless sheep rescued by the faithful shepherd, prefiguring Christ the Good Shepherd who lays down his life for the sheep
slain lion — the predatory power of evil defeated; David's victory foreshadows Christ's triumph over Satan, the roaring lion
raised hand pointing heavenward — thanksgiving and attribution of victory to God, not to human strength
shepherd's tunic — humble pastoral vocation from which God raises up the king of Israel
rocky wilderness — the dangerous terrain of faithfulness, where God proves and prepares His chosen servants
description: A young, barefoot shepherd boy sits triumphantly atop the body of a slain lion whose mouth hangs open in death. The boy cradles a small white lamb tenderly in his right arm while raising his left hand upward toward heaven in a gesture of praise. He wears a simple light-colored tunic. Behind him, a dark and rugged mountainous landscape rises steeply. The lamb nestles peacefully against the boy's chest, contrasting with the massive, lifeless predator beneath him.
theological_description: David's slaying of the lion (and bear) to protect his father's sheep is among the most typologically rich episodes of the Old Testament. In 1 Samuel 17:34-37, David recounts this deed to Saul as proof that God who delivered him from the paw of the lion will also deliver him from Goliath. The Church Fathers — Origen, Augustine, and Gregory the Great — read David the shepherd as a type of Christ the Good Shepherd (John 10:11), who rescues the lost lamb of humanity from the jaws of the devil. The slain lion prefigures Christ's conquest over Satan, whom 1 Peter 5:8 describes as 'a roaring lion seeking someone to devour.' David's raised hand acknowledges that the victory belongs to God alone (1 Samuel 17:47), just as Christ's triumph over death is accomplished by divine power. The lamb cradled against the shepherd's breast echoes Isaiah 40:11: 'He will gather the lambs in his arms.' The youth of David, chosen over his elder brothers, fulfills the biblical pattern of God's preferential election of the lowly (1 Corinthians 1:27). From this shepherd boy God will raise up the royal line from which the Messiah descends (Romans 1:3).

scripture_refs.reason: David tells Saul he killed a lion and bear to rescue his father's sheep
The Lord who delivered me from the paw of the lion will deliver me from the Philistine
I am the Good Shepherd who lays down his life for the sheep — David as Christ-type
The devil prowls like a roaring lion — the slain lion prefigures Satan's defeat
He will gather the lambs in his arms — the tender shepherd cradling the rescued lamb
The Lord is my shepherd — David who shepherds the flock is himself shepherded by God
The Messiah descended from David according to the flesh — the shepherd-king's royal lineage
David the youngest, keeping sheep, chosen by God over his brothers
